- Germany charter destinations, Pakistan/Taliban drone city, DHS replacement, OpenAI investment CEO, Epstein subpoena, Sri Lanka cricket coach, and Nepal prime minister had no new Feb. 28 exact-name signal stronger than existing priors.

## 2026-03-01 Update

Resolution events:
- Fruit-exporter losses resolved to South Africa. I missed by overreacting to India trade/rice coverage. Lesson: when the prompt says "fruit exporters", do not substitute adjacent commodity coverage; include major Southern Hemisphere fruit exporters when shipping lanes are disrupted.
- AWS region disruption resolved to Bahrain. I had Bahrain only as a tail. Lesson: AWS Middle East/Bahrain region can be the answer even when UAE and Israel have higher conflict salience.
- Taliban March drone city resolved to Rawalpindi. The late shift to Rawalpindi/Peshawar was useful and Rawalpindi deserved the top slot.
- Germany charter country resolved to Oman. I missed by anchoring on Jordan/Turkey evacuation routes. Lesson: Oman is not just a mediator; it can become the practical evacuation hub in US-Iran escalation questions.
- F-15E friendly-fire country resolved to Kuwait. Raising Kuwait on Feb. 28 helped but was still too low after the Ali al-Salem signal. Lesson: once one Kuwait base-casualty market resolves to Kuwait, related air-defense/friendly-fire markets should move more aggressively.
- Gulf energy-strike location resolved to Ras Laffan. I missed by staying on Saudi oil infrastructure. Lesson: Qatar/Ras Laffan should be live for Gulf energy markets whenever Qatar is heavily targeted.
- Broadcaster headquarters resolved to IRIB. The formal-name hedge helped, but short exact acronym "IRIB" was sufficient.

Updates made:
- Jebel Ali/Dubai: Al Jazeera and Straits Times reported plumes/smoke at Dubai's Jebel Ali Port after debris from an intercepted missile. Shifted the UAE emirate oil-facility/debris market from Abu Dhabi to Dubai.
- Hormuz tanker: Al Jazeera reported a Palau-flagged oil tanker ablaze near the Strait of Hormuz after an unidentified projectile. Raised Strait of Hormuz to a near-direct forecast.
- Tehran hospital: Reuters/NDTV/HT reported Gandhi Hospital in Tehran was hit. Raised Tehran for the hospital-babies evacuation city while leaving small-city tails because school-strike questions resolved to Minab/Qazvin.
- Qatar/Ras Laffan spillover: the Ras Laffan resolution and Qatar industrial-area fire made Qatar much more plausible for the remaining state-run refinery and plant/power-water facility markets.
- Oman mediation and exposure: Trump said Iran's new leadership wanted talks; HT reported Iran's offer via Oman, and Oman/Duqm/Muscat appeared in attack and embassy-shelter reporting. Raised Oman for Pakistan talks, Trump intermediary, Germany-style evacuation logic, and Oman/Muscat embassy/plant markets.
- Sri Lanka coach: Reuters/Straits Times reported Sanath Jayasuriya intends to step down after the T20 World Cup. Cut Jayasuriya and raised Mahela Jayawardene, Kumar Sangakkara, Marvan Atapattu and Naveed Nawaz for the next men's coach.
- Carrick/Manchester United: United remained unbeaten after beating Crystal Palace. With possible March fixtures before the 14th, moved the first-defeat market toward Liverpool, with "No team" and West Ham/Southampton live.
- School-strike responsibility: March 1 coverage continued to frame the Minab school strike as Israeli/US-Israeli bombing. Raised Israeli military and Israel in the school-strike responsibility/accusation markets.
- OpenAI: fresh funding coverage kept Masayoshi Son/SoftBank as the best $30bn CEO answer; raised him further while leaving Jensen Huang live for Nvidia's parallel $30bn commitment.

Watched but no update:
- DHS replacement, Epstein subpoena, prediction-market co-sponsor/bill-name, Tehran southwest school town, KC-135 damage country, Iranian island bombing, AFC women's fixtures, Ukraine counteroffensive region, and airline financial outlook had no March 1 exact-answer signal strong enough for another move.

## 2026-03-02 Update

Resolution events:
- UAE emirate major oil-facility/plumes/debris resolved to Fujairah. I missed by overfocusing on Dubai/Jebel Ali and Abu Dhabi. Lesson: Fujairah is a key UAE oil/shipping hub and should be live for Gulf infrastructure/debris questions.
- Pakistan ready to bring someone into Iran talks resolved to United States. I missed by reading "bring into talks" as a mediator question. Lesson: the object can be the main counterparty Pakistan hopes to bring into negotiations.
- Trump trade/base-access refusal resolved to Spain. I missed by concentrating on Gulf hosts. Lesson: base-access refusal questions can hit NATO/Europe and Spanish bases such as Rota/Moron.
- Iranian city hospital-babies evacuation resolved to Bushehr. I missed by keeping Tehran too high after Gandhi Hospital. Lesson: exact hospital/location markets can resolve to smaller Gulf/coastal Iranian cities even when Tehran dominates headlines.

Updates made:
- Cargo ship/tanker waterway: Khaleej Times and HT reported MKD VYOM suffered an explosion/fire after a suspected projectile off Muscat, with Oman News Agency placing it in the Gulf of Oman. Shifted q937255 from Strait of Hormuz to Gulf of Oman.
- Saudi refinery: Reuters-derived/Baird/NDTV/HT reporting said Saudi Aramco shut Ras Tanura refinery after Iranian drone/missile debris caused a fire. Raised Saudi Arabia to dominant for the state-run Gulf refinery market.
- Embassy evacuation/shelter: Fox reported the US Embassy in Baghdad urged Americans to shelter in place, while Al Jazeera reported pro-Iran protesters near the US embassy. Added Baghdad as the clear leader for the capital-city US Embassy evacuation market.
- Saudi city projectile: no direct residential-strike naming signal yet, but Ras Tanura/Dammam-area attack increased eastern Saudi risk. Rebalanced toward Riyadh and Dammam rather than only Riyadh.
- Zelenskyy/drone-help request: Reuters/Straits Times said Zelenskyy had no direct requests yet; Al Jazeera said Starmer announced Ukrainian experts would help down drones over Gulf nations. Cut Israel and added Qatar/United Kingdom/UAE/Saudi as the main live interpretations.
- Nepal prime minister: Al Jazeera profiled Balendra Shah as "Nepal's next prime minister?" and said he is positioning himself for the post. Raised Balendra Shah over Gagan Thapa.
- Beirut strikes: Al Jazeera/Irish Times named Dahiyeh, Burj al-Barajneh and Haret Hreik in Beirut's southern suburbs after Israeli strikes. Raised Dahiyeh and added Burj al-Barajneh for the Beirut apartment/district market; raised Hezbollah for the apartment-using organisation.
- DHS replacement: Daily Beast reported Kristi Noem excluded CBP Commissioner Rodney Scott from high-stakes hearing prep. Added Rodney Scott as a small replacement tail while keeping Tom Homan first.
- Epstein subpoena: Feb. 27/March 2 coverage still pointed most directly to Howard Lutnick, with Ro Khanna saying there may be votes to subpoena him. Raised Lutnick over Pam Bondi.
- Power/water plant country: Qatar's Mesaieed water tank at a power plant and Kuwait's West Doha Power and Water Station were better matches than Oman's port/tanker casualties. Raised Qatar and Kuwait, cut Oman.

Watched but no update:
- Senator/protester, Carrick first defeat, OpenAI $30bn CEO, Tehran southwest school town, DOD school-strike responsibility, Merkley co-sponsor, anti-cartel coalition, Sri Lanka cricket coach, Ukraine counteroffensive region, airline financial outlook, and Iraqi militia casualties had no March 2 exact-name signal strong enough for a fresh move.

## 2026-03-03 Update

Resolution events:
- Senator helping remove anti-war protester resolved to Tim Sheehy. I missed by focusing on Armed Services seniority/foreign-policy hawks. Lesson: include newer veteran senators on protest/removal questions, not only committee leaders.
- Carrick first defeat resolved to Newcastle United. I missed by relying on likely Liverpool/West Ham fixtures. Lesson: football fixture-path markets need broader schedule coverage; "first defeat by date" can be a less salient domestic opponent.
- OpenAI $30bn CEO resolved to Jensen Huang. I had him as a tail but over-weighted SoftBank/Masayoshi Son. Lesson: when the wording says "his company is set to invest another $30bn" and "last investment until IPO", Nvidia/Huang can fit better than SoftBank even when SoftBank also has a $30bn commitment.
- Zelenskyy drone-help country resolved to United States. I missed after moving to Qatar/UK. Lesson: "made a request" can mean the US asked Ukraine to share help for Middle East drone defence, even if public coverage frames Gulf nations as beneficiaries.
- Epstein subpoena resolved to Pam Bondi. I had her as a tail but overreacted to the Howard Lutnick vote quote. Lesson: when a question says Justice Department handling of records, the attorney general should usually remain first unless there is an official subpoena vote naming someone else.
- Cargo ship waterway resolved to Strait of Hormuz. I hurt the forecast by moving from direct Hormuz reporting to a related Gulf of Oman tanker article. Lesson: keep the exact question wording and first direct article at the center; adjacent shipping incidents can be separate markets.

Updates made:
- US Embassy capital city: March 3 Al Jazeera/AFP-derived reporting said two drones hit the US Embassy in Saudi capital Riyadh, and the embassy issued shelter-in-place notices for Riyadh/Jeddah/Dhahran. Shifted the capital-city embassy market from Baghdad to Riyadh.
- Saudi residential/projectile city: Riyadh diplomatic-quarter/US Embassy attack and intercepted drones near Riyadh and Al-Kharj made Riyadh dominant and Al-Kharj a live tail for the Saudi city projectile market.
- Minab school responsibility: Al Jazeera's investigation described the Minab school strike as occurring during initial US-Israel strikes, while US and Israeli officials denied awareness. Kept Israeli military first but raised U.S. military and U.S.-Israeli coalition.
- DHS replacement: Noem faced severe bipartisan/MAGA criticism in Senate testimony; Tom Homan remained the operational fallback in coverage. Raised Homan slightly.
- Prediction-market co-sponsor: Benzinga reported Chris Murphy pushing prediction-market legislation after Iran attack trading. Raised Murphy further for the Merkley co-introducer market.
- Low-coverage open-answer markets near deadline: broadened anti-cartel coalition name, southwest-Tehran school town, Trump "region taken over", tornado-death state, Beirut coastal district, southern Lebanon district, and Oslo embassy forecasts to include plausible tails instead of leaving too much probability on one or two names.
- Beirut districts: March 3 evacuation warnings named Ghobeiry and Haret Hreik, while Dahiyeh remained the broader southern-suburb label. Rebalanced the apartment/district market from Dahiyeh-only toward Haret Hreik/Ghobeiry.

Watched but no update:
- Saudi refinery country stayed Saudi Arabia after Ras Tanura evidence; Nepal PM stayed Balendra Shah after another Gen Z/RSP profile; Sri Lanka cricket coach, airline financial outlook, Ukraine counteroffensive region, Iraqi militia casualties, KC-135 country, World Cup venue country, and Trump-Iran intermediary had no March 3 exact-answer signal strong enough for a fresh move.

## 2026-03-04 Update

Resolution events:
- DHS replacement resolved to Markwayne Mullin. I missed by treating the Noem backlash as pointing to immigration administrators (Homan/Scott) rather than a Trump-aligned senator. Lesson: cabinet replacement questions can resolve to loyal political validators, not only agency operators.
- Anti-cartel coalition resolved to Americas Counter-Cartel Coalition. My broadened exact-name list was close but used Anti-Cartel instead of Counter-Cartel. Lesson: for invented official names, include "Counter-" variants when the domain is counternarcotics/countercartel.
- US Embassy evacuation capital resolved to Doha. I missed after overreacting to the Riyadh embassy attack. Lesson: "homes near embassy ordered evacuated" is a distinct condition from embassy shelter-in-place/attack; Qatar/Doha remains high-signal in this conflict.
- Saudi city projectile resolved to Al-Kharj. Adding Al-Kharj helped, but it was still underweighted. Lesson: when Saudi reports name Riyadh and Al-Kharj together, Al-Kharj can be the specific residential/civilian strike answer.
- State-run oil refinery resolved to Bahrain. I missed badly by anchoring on Ras Tanura. Lesson: Gulf refinery questions can resolve to Bahrain/BAPCO/Sitra even when Saudi Aramco has louder coverage.
- Merkley co-sponsor resolved to Amy Klobuchar. I missed by following the separate Chris Murphy prediction-market bill. Lesson: ethics bills about officials/trading often pair Merkley with Klobuchar-style good-government sponsors, not only Warren/Murphy.
- DOD school-strike responsibility resolved to US military. Raising U.S. military helped, though it should have been first once the DOD investigation framing appeared.
- Southwest Tehran town resolved to Parand. Adding Parand as a tail helped. Lesson: Tehran satellite-town markets need Parand, Shahriar, Robat Karim, Eslamshahr, Qods.

Updates made:
- Nepal PM: Hindustan Times said Balen/Balendra Shah is the RSP's projected prime minister candidate, drew huge crowds, and is the most followed race. Raised Balendra Shah sharply and added Ravi Lamichhane as an RSP institutional tail.
- Trump "region taken over": no exact quote yet, but March 4 coverage continues to frame the war as remaking the Middle East and spreading across Gulf states. Raised Middle East modestly above Persian Gulf.
- Australia missiles: no new exact article, but the country recipient is overwhelmingly likely Ukraine; raised Ukraine and added small strategic-partner tails.
- Airline financial outlook: no exact article yet; broadened from Qantas/Lufthansa to include Air France-KLM, IAG, and Delta as listed airlines exposed to fuel-cost shocks.
- Prediction-market bill name: with Murphy/Casar bill still unresolved, broadened exact-title guesses to include public-integrity/sensitive-event/event-contract language.

Watched but no update:
- Tornado-death state, southern Lebanon district, Minab school country-accusation market, Beirut hotel/apartment districts, Oslo embassy, Sri Lanka coach, Ukraine counteroffensive region, Iraqi militia casualties, World Cup venue country, KC-135 base country, and Hormuz participation/refusal had no March 4 exact-answer signal strong enough for another move.

## 2026-03-05 Update

Resolution events:
- Trump "region taken over" resolved to Middle East. Raising Middle East on March 4 worked.
- Tornado-death state outside Oklahoma resolved to Michigan. I missed by using central-plains/tornado-alley priors. Lesson: outbreak fatality questions can include Great Lakes states; add Michigan/Indiana/Ohio when "central U.S." storms broaden eastward.

Updates made:
- Nepal PM: March 5 Reuters/Straits Times/Al Jazeera/Irish Times coverage called RSP the frontrunner, named Balendra Shah as its prime ministerial candidate, and described him drawing large youth crowds. Raised Balendra Shah to a strong favorite and kept Gagan Thapa/Ravi Lamichhane as tails.
- School-accusation country: after q887 resolved to US military responsibility, shifted the related "which country did the United States previously accuse" market toward Iran while keeping Israel and United States as hedges because March 5 reporting still focused on US-Israel denials and Iran's accusations.

Watched but no update:
- Southern Lebanon district, Beirut hotel/apartment districts, Oslo embassy, Sri Lanka cricket coach, Australia missiles, airline financial outlook, Ukraine counteroffensive region, Iran World Cup venue country, Iraqi militia casualties, AFC women's quarterfinals, Liverpool UCL scorer, IPL opener, medical-device cyberattack persona, and central Beirut apartment organisation had no March 5 exact-answer signal strong enough for another move.

## 2026-03-06 Update

Resolution events:
- Southern Lebanon district resolved to Tyre district. I had Tyre as a tail but not first. Lesson: for southern Lebanon building/strike district markets, include "Tyre district" exactly, not only the city name "Tyre".
- Nepal PM resolved to Balendra Shah. The strong March 5 move was correct.
- School-accusation country resolved to Iran. The March 5 conditional shift after US-military responsibility was correct.

Updates made:
- Oslo embassy markets: no direct Oslo reporting, but March 6 JPost/NSC reporting warned of concrete attack concerns for Israelis abroad and synagogue/Israeli-target incidents. Raised Israeli Embassy/Israel modestly.
- Beirut coastal hotel district: no exact hotel-room report yet; raised Raouche as the likeliest coastal hotel district while keeping Manara/Ain el-Mreisseh/Ramlet al-Baida/Minet el-Hosn live.

Watched but no update:
- Beirut apartment district, Sri Lanka cricket coach, Australia missile recipient, airline outlook, Ukraine counteroffensive region, Iran World Cup venue country, Iraqi militia casualties, China AFC quarterfinal opponent, Liverpool UCL scorer, IPL opener, PSG-Chelsea substitute scorer, medical-device cyberattack persona, Real Madrid-Man City hat-trick, central Beirut apartment organisation, Detroit synagogue, and western Iraq aircraft model had no March 6 exact-answer signal strong enough for another move.

    best=[]

        hits=[t for t in toks if t in text]

PERTH – A ruthless Japan thrashed India 11-0 to reach the Women’s Asian Cup quarter-finals thanks to hat-tricks from Hinata Miyazawa and Riko Ueki on March 7. Group C leaders Japan, champions in 2014 and 2018, cut through India’s hapless defence with ease while the south Asian side failed to have a single shot in the game that was played predominantly in India’s half at Perth Rectangular Stadium. Japan overwhelmed India in the first half and were 5-0 up at half-time with a superb curling effort from Yuzuki Yamamoto in the fourth minute, a Yui Hasegawa goal, a brace from Miyazawa and a Kiko Seike penalty. With a comfortable lead, Japan made three changes at half-time but they did not relent as substitute Riko Ueki scored twice in three minutes before completing an 18-minute hat-trick after Maya Hijikata also got on the scoresheet. Miyazawa capped off the rout by completing her own hat-trick in the 81st minute, sealing an 11-0 scoreline that highlighted the gulf in class between Asia’s highest-ranked team in the world and their hapless opponents 59 rungs below them. Earlier on March 7, Chinese Taipei held off a late Vietnam push to secure a 1-0 victory and their first win of the tournament at the same stadium. Su Yu-hsuan’s first-half header gave the three-time Asian champions the lead, with both sides otherwise struggling to find cohesion in attack before the break in temperatures approaching 40 deg C. Vietnam, quarter-finalists at the previous edition, had nine attempts on goal but none on target, while Taiwan stayed organised at the back to preserve their advantage in the Group C tie. A late video assistant referee review for a possible penalty for Vietnam did not yield a decision, and Taiwan moved provisionally into second place in Group C on three points, behind Japan who are on a maximum six points. Vietnam are third, also on three points, with India bottom with no points. Meanwhile, an Iranian state television presenter has labelled the women’s national football team as “wartime traitors” after the players did not sing their national anthem before the Asian Cup game against South Korea in Australia. Iran are playing in the continental tournament even as a military conflict escalates back home after the US and Israel launched air strikes over the weekend, killing the Islamic Republic’s Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei. Their players stood in silence when Iran’s anthem was played before their 3-0 loss to South Korea on March 2, though they sang and saluted b

LINE 1584 DATE_PUBLISH 2026-03-07 SOURCE www.hindustantimes.com TITLE AFC Women’s Asian Cup: Japan rout India 11-0 for 4th worst loss URL https://www.hindustantimes.com/sports/football/afc-women-s-asian-cup-japan-rout-india-11-0-for-4th-worst-loss-101772899676548.html
Kolkata: Japan’s 11-0 win against India in the AFC Women’s Asian Cup in Perth on Saturday reflected where the countries are in their football journey. As the former world champions and twice winners of this competition cruised into the quarter-finals, India careered towards the exit having qualified to be among the continent’s top 12 teams for the first time. How India lift themselves against Chinese Taipei on Tuesday from the biggest defeat of the competition is possibly the only point of interest remaining in their campaign. A contest between professional and part-timers is likely to be lop-sided. Not so lop-sided to be India’s fourth worst defeat, and the biggest since a 12-0 loss to China in 2003, they would have hoped especially after the spirited performance against Vietnam. But hope is not a strategy. In four minutes India found that out as Yuzuki Yamamoto curled a left-footer into the far corner. What followed was Panthoi Chanu’s Elangbam’s goal being used for target practice as Manchester United’s Hinata Miyazawa (20, 35, 81) and substitute Riko Ueki (47, 50, 65)of West Ham, who scored with her first touch, notched up hattricks. Kiko Seike (45+5, 55) got a brace including a penalty and skipper and Manchester City midfielder Yui Hasegawa (13) and Maya Hijikata (62) scored once each. import pandas as pd, json, re
from pathlib import Path
stop=set('which what where when will with from that this have has been were says said into about after before during through whose over under between country team city state official officials reported reporting public source truth major question resolve resolves resolution criteria accepted answer format provide exact only later than first date title background current expected name organization organisation identify identified march 2026 2025 start source using used use made make would could should also while because being whose whose'.split())
stop |= set('the and for are was its his her they them their who how one two three four five six seven eight nine ten more some other only full short long news major reports report publicly'.split())
stop |= set('united states donald trump iran iranian israeli israel american u.s. march february january april'.split())
# specific generic active words
stop |= set('resolution criteria answer format accepted authoritative directly statement statements source truth no later english public resolving'.split())

def toks(s):
    out=[]
    for t in re.findall(r\"[A-Za-z][A-Za-z0-9'’-]+\", str(s).lower()):
        t=t.strip(\"'’-\")
        if len(t)>=5 and t not in stop:
            out.append(t)
    return list(dict.fromkeys(out))
arts=[]
for i,line in enumerate(open('articles/2026/03/07/articles.jsonl',encoding='utf-8'),1):
    a=json.loads(line); dp=(a.get('date_publish') or '')[:10]
    if dp and dp>'2026-03-07': continue
    text=((a.get('title') or '')+'\\n'+(a.get('content') or '')).lower()
    arts.append((i,a,text))
df=pd.read_csv('market.csv', dtype=str)
act=df[df.is_resolved.astype(str).str.lower()=='false'].copy()
results=[]
for _,r in act.iterrows():
    qtext=' '.join(str(r.get(c,'')) for c in ['title','background','resolution_criteria','market_aggregate'])
    qtokens=toks(qtext)
    best=[]
    for i,a,text in arts:
        hits=[t for t in qtokens if t in text]
        if len(hits)>=3:
            title=(a.get('title') or '').lower()
            score=len(hits)+2*sum(1 for t in hits if t in title)
            # penalize common generic-only hits by requiring one less-common phrase in title/content?
            best.append((score,len(hits),i,a,hits,text))
    if best:
        best.sort(reverse=True,key=lambda x:x[0])
        if best[0][0] >= 12 or best[0][1] >= 5:
            results.append((pd.to_datetime(r.resolution_date), r.qid, r.title, best[:2]))
for rd,qid,title,best in sorted(results)[:80]:
    print('\\nQ',qid,'resolve',rd.date(), title)
    for score,nh,i,a,hits,text in best:
        print(' ',score,nh,'line',i,'date',a.get('date_publish'),'src',a.get('source'),'title:',a.get('title'))
        print('  hits:',hits[:10])
PY"

LINE 297 DATE 2026-03-07 SRC www.straitstimes.com TITLE Iran war: Hit to global energy markets URL https://www.straitstimes.com/world/iran-war-threatens-a-prolonged-hit-to-global-energy-markets
Follow our live coverage here. DUBAI – The war with Iran could leave consumers and businesses worldwide facing weeks or months of higher fuel prices even if the week-old conflict ends quickly, as suppliers grapple with damaged facilities, disrupted logistics, and elevated risks to shipping. The outlook poses a wider global economic threat, as well as a political vulnerability for US President Donald Trump leading into the midterm elections, with voters sensitive to energy bills and unfavourable to foreign entanglements. “The market is shifting from pricing pure geopolitical risk to grappling with tangible operational disruption, as refinery shutdowns and export constraints begin to impair crude processing and regional supply flows,” JP Morgan analysts said in a research note on March 6. The conflict has already led to the suspension of around a fifth of global crude and natural gas supply, as Tehran targets ships in the vital Strait of Hormuz between its shores and Oman, and attacks energy infrastructure across the region. Global oil prices have surged 24 per cent this week to over US$90 (S$115) a barrel and are on course for their steepest weekly gains since the pandemic, driving up fuel prices for consumers worldwide. A nearly complete shutdown of the Strait means the region’s giant oil producers – Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates, Iraq and Kuwait – have had to suspend shipments of as much as 140 million barrels of oil, or equal to about 1.4 days of global demand, to global refiners. As a result, oil and gas storage at facilities in the Middle East are rapidly filling, forcing oil fields in Iraq to cut oil production, and Kuwait and the United Arab Emirates most likely to cut next, analysts, traders and sources said. “At some point soon, everyone will also shut in if vessels do not come,” said a ⁠source with a state oil company in the region, who asked not to be named. Oil fields forced to shut across the Middle East as a result of the shipping disruptions could take a while to return to normal, said Mr Amir Zaman, head of the Americas commercial team at Rystad Energy. “The conflict could be ended, but it could take days or weeks or months, depending on 

LINE 431 DATE 2026-03-07 SRC www.straitstimes.com TITLE Israel carries out rare airborne raid in Lebanon; 16 killed in strikes URL https://www.straitstimes.com/world/middle-east/israel-carries-out-rare-airborne-raid-in-lebanon-16-killed-in-strikes
– Israel’s military carried out an airborne operation that dropped troops into a town in eastern Lebanon overnight, residents and Lebanese state media said on March 7, as heavy Israeli strikes on the area left more than a dozen people dead. Lebanon was plunged deeper into the conflict engulfing the Middle East on March 2 when Iran-backed Hezbollah launched rockets and drones into Israel, which has responded with heavy bombardment across Lebanon’s south, east and near the capital Beirut. Overnight, Israeli helicopters dropped troops near the town of Nabi Chit in Lebanon’s eastern Bekaa Valley, Lebanese state media and residents said. Residents and Hezbollah fighters fired on the troops as they moved on foot, the reports said. The Israeli troops withdrew and left by helicopter, as Israeli strikes pounded Nabi Chit and nearby towns, state media and residents said. Lebanon’s Health Ministry said 16 people had been killed in Israeli attacks in the last 24 hours in Nabi Chit. A spokesman said the toll included people killed in strikes throughout March 6, as well as during intense overnight bombardment, and that it was expected to rise further. Two security sources said three army troops were among the dead. Israeli strikes have killed more than 200 people across Lebanon, and orders to evacuate have displaced around 300,000 people, only a third of whom are now living in government shelters. ‘A night of hell’ Mr Shawki al-Masri, who lives in a town adjacent to Nabi Chit, described the overnight bombing as “a night of hell”. “We heard the helicopters over our house all night. They were so low we thought they would land on us,” he said. “People in the town woke up and started shooting at them, then the warplanes started bombing. It was a very violent night and only calmed down when the sun came up,” he said. In a statement overnight, Hezbollah said it fired on Israeli troops who were dropped near Nabi Chit by four Israeli helicopters. The group said the Israeli troops withdrew. While Israeli forces are carrying out ground operations in southern Lebanon, pushing deeper into a strip of border villages, airborne operations are rare. In a 2024 war between Israel and Hezbollah

LINE 463 DATE 2026-03-07 SRC www.aljazeera.com TITLE Hezbollah, Israeli troops clash on the ground in eastern Lebanon’s Bekaa URL https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2026/3/7/hezbollah-israeli-troops-clash-on-the-ground-in-eastern-lebanons-bekaa
Clashes have erupted as Israeli forces attempted a landing operation along the Lebanon-Syria border, with the armed group Hezbollah saying its fighters were involved, according to Lebanese state-run National News Agency (NNA), as this fierce front in the wider regional war launched by the United States and Israel boils over. The Lebanese Health Ministry says at least 16 people were killed and 35 were wounded in Israeli air raids on the town of Nabi Chit, in the eastern Bekaa Valley. Recommended Stories list of 4 items list 1 of 4Anti-aircraft fire seen in eastern Lebanon amid Israeli incursion list 2 of 4Thousands of Syrian refugees flee Lebanon after Israeli strikes list 3 of 4Iran’s legal case for striking the Gulf collapses under scrutiny list 4 of 4Iran’s strikes on the Gulf: Burning the bridges of good neighbourliness end of list There was no immediate comment from the Israeli military, which has launched numerous deadly attacks and sent ground troops into southern Lebanon, but not further north in the east, since the Iran-aligned Lebanese group Hezbollah fired missiles at Israel on Monday to avenge the killing of Iranian Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, joining the war. This latest reported raid would be the deepest Israeli forces’ incursion inside Lebanon since special unit troops apprehended Hezbollah operative Imad Amhaz from the northern city of Batroun in November 2024. The NNA said, “Clashes are taking place on the eastern mountain range along the Lebanese-Syrian border … to repel Israeli landing attempts.” ‘Infiltration from the Syrian direction’ The agency gave the location of the incursion as Nabi Chit, in the eastern Baalbek district where Hezbollah holds sway. Hezbollah said in a statement that its fighters had “observed the infiltration of four Israeli enemy army helicopters from the Syrian direction”. After landing, advancing troops “were engaged by a group” of Hezbollah fighters as they reached the Nabi Chit cemetery, Hezbollah said, noting the use of light and medium weapons. “The clash escalated after the enemy force was exposed,” it added, saying the Israeli troops launched intense strikes and began to evacuate. LINE 451 DATE 2026-03-07 SRC www.aljazeera.com TITLE Why Yemen’s Houthis are staying out of Israel-US fight with Iran - for now URL https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2026/3/7/why-yemens-houthis-are-staying-out-of-israel-us-fight-with-iran-for-now
Sanaa, Yemen – The Israel-US attacks on Iran have plunged the wider Middle East region into turmoil. From Tehran, tensions have spread, affecting multiple Arab cities, including Abu Dhabi, Doha, Kuwait City, Manama, and Beirut. Amid this wave of military escalation hitting several countries, Yemen has remained – perhaps surprisingly – quiet. The Houthi movement, the Iran-allied de facto authority in northwestern Yemen, has made repeated attacks on US and Israeli targets since the start of Israel’s genocidal war on Gaza in October 2023. Recommended Stories list of 3 items list 1 of 3The end of Iran’s strategic patience list 2 of 3A weak Iran would backfire on the United States list 3 of 3A long and troubled trail: Qatar’s IRGC cell arrests strain Iran relations end of list But in the week since the US-Israeli attacks on Iran began on February 28, the Houthis have limited their support for Tehran to rhetoric and a mass protest denouncing the strikes. Whether they will continue to remain detached from the conflict remains uncertain. Analysts say the rebel group’s involvement is still possible, and its current restraint appears part of a strategy of patience. “Houthi intervention remains a possibility, and it could take the form of a phased escalation,” Luca Nevola, senior analyst for Yemen and the Gulf at the ACLED conflict monitor, told Al Jazeera. “At the current stage, the main Houthi priority remains avoiding direct US and Israeli retaliation.” Last August, Israeli strikes killed at least 12 Houthi high-ranking government members, including Prime Minister Ahmed al-Rahawi and chief of staff Mohammed al-Ghumari, in air strikes in Sanaa. The losses were among the heaviest the group has endured during its confrontation with the US and Israel. That incident, coupled with other attacks last year, has left the Houthi leadership more careful and wary of risking a heavy aerial campaign on areas under its control. “The group seems to fear Israeli intelligence and the possibility of leadership decapitation,” Nevola said. Despite the losses the Houthi group endured last year, it is not entirely incapacitated, and it could launch assaults on adversaries. Nevola explained, “

LINE 483 DATE 2026-03-07 SRC www.aljazeera.com TITLE UAE president says prepared to confront ‘threats’ as Iran attacks continue URL https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2026/3/7/uae-president-says-prepared-to-confront-threats-as-iran-attacks-continue
The president of the United Arab Emirates spoke for the first time on the widening war in the Middle East as Iran continues to strike Gulf countries hosting US military assets with drones and missiles. “The UAE has thick skin and bitter flesh – we are no easy prey,” said Sheikh Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan in comments aired by Abu Dhabi TV on Saturday as he visited wounded patients in a hospital. Recommended Stories list of 4 items list 1 of 4Iran to halt strikes on neighbours unless attacks from there: Pezeshkian list 2 of 4Iranian Kurd leader in Iraq says ground operation into Iran ‘highly likely’ list 3 of 4Iranian authorities warn against ‘fifth column’, as no signs of war abating list 4 of 4War against Iran: How far will it go? end of list He added the UAE is in “a period of war” but would “emerge stronger”. In a social media post, Sheikh Mohamed said the UAE, which has seen attacks affecting hubs such as airports, tourist attractions, and the US consulate in Dubai, is prepared to confront “threats” against the “security and the protection of all citizens”. One driver was killed when debris from an intercepted projectile slammed into his vehicle, Dubai’s Media Office said, describing the victim as Asian but providing no further details. Sheikh Mohamed’s comments were aired as the region entered a second week of war sparked by a major US-Israeli attack on Iran. Earlier, Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian offered an apology to neighbouring nations for launching strikes on their countries housing US military bases. His comments were swiftly contradicted by Iranian judiciary chief Gholamhossein Mohseni Ejei, also a member of the interim leadership council. “Evidence from Iran’s armed forces shows that the geography of some countries in the region is openly and covertly at the disposal of the enemy,” he said. “The heavy attacks on these targets will continue.” Pezeshkian himself rolled back on his remarks that Gulf countries would not be targeted unless attacks originated from their territories, caveating that while his country emphasised “the preservation and continuation of friendly relations,” Iran still has an “inherent right” to defend itself against US-Isr

LINE 445 DATE 2026-03-07 SRC www.aljazeera.com TITLE ‘They’re cancer’: Trump threatens cartels, Cuba at Latin American summit URL https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2026/3/7/theyre-a-cancer-trump-threatens-cartels-cuba-at-latin-american-summit?traffic_source=rss
At the inaugural “Shield of the Americas” summit in South Florida, United States President Donald Trump announced the creation of what he calls the Americas Counter-Cartel Coalition: a group of a dozen politically aligned countries committed to fighting drug trafficking. But as he signed a declaration to cement that commitment, Trump signalled that it came with the expectation that cartels would not be confronted with law enforcement action, but instead military might. You’re going to use your military.” Saturday’s summit was the latest step in a larger foreign policy pivot under Trump. Since taking office for a second term, Trump has distanced himself from some of the US’s traditional allies in Europe, instead forging tighter partnerships with right-wing governments around the world. The attendance at the Shield of the Americas summit reflected that shift. Right-wing leaders, including Argentina’s Javier Milei, El Salvador’s Nayib Bukele and Ecuador’s Daniel Noboa, were among the guest list. But notably absent was top-level leadership from Mexico, the US’s biggest trading partner, and Brazil, the largest country in the region by economy and population. Both Mexico and Brazil are led by left-wing presidents who have resisted some of Trump’s more hardline policies. The growing rift between the US and some of its longtime partners was a feature in the brief remarks delivered by US Secretary of State Marco Rubio, who praised attendees for their cooperation. “They’re more than allies. FILE articles/2026/03/02/articles.jsonl LINE 379 DATE 2026-03-02 SRC www.khaleejtimes.com TITLE Football World Cup tickets: Huge demand and sky-high prices URL https://www.khaleejtimes.com/sports/football/football-world-cup-tickets-huge-demand-and-sky-high-prices
From almost $900 for the opening game to over $8,000 for the final, match tickets are far from cheap for the World Cup which begins in North America in 100 days. AFP Sport assesses the cost of attending games at the tournament: How many tickets, how much demand? According to world football's governing body Fifa, almost seven million tickets have been made available in total. Each person can buy a maximum of four tickets per match and a total of 40 for the whole competition. Around two million tickets were sold during an initial lottery in October. A second sales phase in December and January was also a lottery and attracted a record 508 million requests, according to Fifa. It has not said how many tickets have been attributed but the matches in highest demand were the Group K finale between Colombia and Portugal in Miami on June 27, co-hosts Mexico against South Korea in Guadalajara on June 18, and the final at the MetLife Stadium in New Jersey on July 19. Prices skyrocket... There will be a total of 104 matches played at this expanded World Cup, the first ever to feature 48 teams, and ticket prices have soared for the most in-demand games. Fan group Football Supporters Europe said the North American bid had initially promised tickets would be available from as little as $21, but instead the cheapest tickets to go on sale have been for $60, for example for the Group J opener between Austria and Jordan at the Levi's Stadium in California. Most tickets cost at least $200 for matches involving leading nations, while the cheapest tickets for the final cost $2,000 and the best seats $8,680 —that is before taking into account Fifa's official resale site, where one category three seat for the game at the MetLife Stadium on July 19 was being advertised for an eye-watering $143,

FILE articles/2026/03/02/articles.jsonl LINE 543 DATE 2026-03-02 SRC www.irishtimes.com TITLE Five-star Ireland get World Cup qualifying campaign off to flying start URL https://www.irishtimes.com/sport/hockey/2026/03/02/five-star-ireland-get-world-cup-qualifying-campaign-off-to-flying-start/
World Cup qualifier: Ireland 5 Malaysia 0 Ireland got their World Cup qualifying campaign off to a winning start in Chile on Monday as three goals in six third-quarter minutes sent them on their way to an emphatic victory over Malaysia. It took them 20 minutes to turn their superiority into a lead against a nation eight places below them in the world rankings, but once they did they were rampant. A beautiful opening goal it was too, Katie Mullan deftly deflecting Hannah McLoughlin’s long ball into the circle past the Malaysian goalkeeper. Three minutes later it was 2-0 when Caoimhe Perdue converted Ireland’s fifth penalty corner of the game, and Sarah McAuley extended the lead further early in the third quarter with a sublime piece of skill. After expertly controlling an aerial ball, she turned and made her way past two defenders before firing a thunderous reverse strike into the bottom right corner from the edge of the circle. Michelle Carey made it 4-0 soon after when she was set up by her twin Niamh, before Róisín Upton got Ireland’s fifth when she swept home from a corner. Upton later hit the crossbar from a penalty stroke. Next up for Ireland are Japan (6.45pm Irish time on Tuesday) who beat Canada 4-0 in the same pool on Monday. The top three teams in the eight-nation tournament qualify for the World Cup, which will be hosted by Belgium and the Netherlands in August.

FILE articles/2026/03/02/articles.jsonl LINE 546 DATE 2026-03-02 SRC www.irishtimes.com TITLE Nothing in Fifa guidebook for when World Cup host attacks participant URL https://www.irishtimes.com/sport/2026/03/02/nothing-in-fifa-guidebook-for-when-world-cup-host-attacks-participant/
There is, Ken Early notes, “nothing in the Fifa guidebook to govern what happens when a World Cup host attacks a participant”, so the governing body’s president, Gianni Infantino, might be finding the United States’ assault on Iran, with the assistance of Israel, a touch on the awkward side. Iran are scheduled to play in Los Angeles and Seattle in this summer’s tournament, but now that Infantino’s peace prize laureate has declared war on them, what next? ‘The show must go on’ took on a whole new meaning in Dubai on Saturday when racing’s Super Saturday fixture went ahead. “Pretty surreal,” as English trainer Jamie Osborne described it. “You’re standing in the paddock watching missiles get shot through the sky.” Brian O’Connor reports on the efforts of the Irish contingent who attended the meeting to get out of Dubai.
